subject: Constitutional Law
module_title: Fundamental Rights II: Family, Education and Children

questions.question: Article 42A was inserted by the 31st Amendment to the Constitution in 2012. What is the primary constitutional significance of Art. 42A.1?

options.A: It creates a new court exclusively for children's cases
options.B: It provides for the first time an express constitutional recognition of children as holders of rights in their own right — distinct from and independent of their parents' rights under Art. 41 and 42
options.C: It replaces the marital family protection of Art. 41 with a universal family rights provision
options.D: It creates a constitutional right for all children to attend State schools

questions.explanation: Art. 42A.1 provides that 'The State recognises and affirms the natural and imprescriptible rights of all children and shall, as far as practicable, by its laws protect and vindicate those rights.' This is constitutionally significant because, prior to the 31st Amendment, children's rights were largely derived from parental rights under Art. 41 and 42, or from the unenumerated rights jurisprudence. Art. 42A gives children constitutional personhood independent of their parents' status — establishing an express constitutional platform for children's rights regardless of whether their parents are married.

questions.question: Article 42A.2.1° provides that in all proceedings concerning the welfare of a child, the court shall regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration. How does this modify the pre-2012 constitutional position established in cases like In re JH (An Infant) [1985]?

options.A: It has no practical effect — courts always applied a best interests test before 2012
options.B: It elevates the child's best interests to a constitutional level paramount consideration, qualifying the strong parental rights presumption from In re JH; courts can no longer simply defer to Art. 41 parental rights without conducting a genuine best interests assessment
options.C: It abolishes Art. 41 parental rights entirely in custody proceedings
options.D: It requires all children's court cases to be heard by the Supreme Court

questions.explanation: Pre-2012 cases like In re JH [1985] required a very high threshold of parental failure before a court could override Art. 41 marital parental rights in favour of a child's welfare. Art. 42A.2.1° constitutionalises the best interests principle as paramount in child welfare proceedings, modifying this framework. Courts must now genuinely assess the child's best interests as the primary constitutional value — not merely ask whether parents have failed at a high threshold. This shifts the analysis: parental rights remain important but are no longer automatically determinative. The Children and Family Relationships Act 2015 further implemented Art. 42A.

questions.question: Article 42A.2.2° provides that in proceedings where the safety or welfare of a child is at risk, a court shall have regard to the child's views. Is there a constitutional obligation to hear the child's voice in all proceedings?

options.A: Yes — every child regardless of age must be heard directly by the judge in all proceedings
options.B: Art. 42A.2.2° requires courts to have regard to the child's views having regard to the child's age and maturity — this creates a qualified obligation that courts must consider the child's views but may do so through various mechanisms, not necessarily direct testimony
options.C: Only children over the age of 16 have a constitutional right to be heard in court proceedings
options.D: Art. 42A.2.2° applies only to criminal proceedings involving children

questions.explanation: Art. 42A.2.2° provides that in proceedings where the safety or welfare of any child is at risk, the court shall, having regard to the age and maturity of the child, give such weight to the ascertainable views of the child as the court deems appropriate and just. This is not a requirement to hear the child directly in all cases — courts have discretion regarding the mechanism for ascertaining views (reports, guardians ad litem, direct evidence depending on maturity). The provision implements the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child Art. 12 (right to be heard) at constitutional level.

questions.question: Article 42A.3 provides that the State shall enact legislation providing for the adoption of any child where the parents have failed in their duty for such period and to such an extent as may be prescribed. What does this provision enable that was previously constitutionally difficult?

options.A: It enables the adoption of children of married parents without their consent, where those parents have failed in their duty for a prescribed period
options.B: It provides for the automatic adoption of all non-marital children
options.C: It enables the adoption of children against the wishes of unmarried fathers only
options.D: It provides a constitutional basis for international adoption from non-Hague Convention states

questions.explanation: Art. 42A.3 resolved a long-standing constitutional obstacle: prior to the 31st Amendment, Art. 41 rights of married parents meant it was constitutionally very difficult (if not impossible) to adopt a child of a married couple without their consent — even where they had effectively abandoned the child. Art. 42A.3 expressly provides a constitutional basis for legislation allowing the adoption of a child of married parents (or any child) without parental consent, where those parents have failed in their duty for a prescribed period. The Adoption (Amendment) Act 2017 implemented this provision, enabling non-consensual adoption in appropriate circumstances.

questions.question: Article 42A.4.1° requires the State to legislate to provide for mandatory reporting of child abuse and neglect. Prior to the Children First Act 2015, the legal and constitutional position regarding mandatory reporting was that:

options.A: Mandatory reporting was already required by Art. 42 and failure to report child abuse was always a criminal offence
options.B: Reporting of child abuse was voluntary for most professionals; mandatory reporting was constitutionally contested on grounds of confidentiality but Art. 42A.4.1° provided the constitutional basis for statutory mandatory reporting, implemented by the Children First Act 2015
options.C: Mandatory reporting requirements were prohibited under the constitutional right to privacy
options.D: Only medical professionals were constitutionally obliged to report child abuse prior to 2012

questions.explanation: Before the Children First Act 2015 (which gave full effect to Art. 42A.4.1°'s mandate), professional reporting of child abuse in Ireland was largely voluntary, governed by administrative guidelines. Art. 42A.4.1°'s requirement that the State legislate for mandatory reporting provided the constitutional imperative for the 2015 Act, which creates legal obligations for mandated persons (teachers, doctors, social workers, etc.) to report knowledge or reasonable grounds for concern about child abuse or neglect. Art. 42A resolved any constitutional tension between professional confidentiality (e.g., legal professional privilege concerns) and child protection obligations.

questions.question: In FN (A Minor) v Minister for Education [1995] 1 IR 409, a case decided before Art. 42A, the High Court (Kelly J) ordered the State to fund a residential placement for a disturbed child with special needs. How does Art. 42A affect the constitutional basis for such orders?

options.A: Art. 42A has no effect on orders regarding special needs placements — such orders continue to be based solely on Art. 42.5
options.B: Art. 42A provides a stronger and more direct constitutional basis for orders requiring the State to vindicate children's rights — a child's best interests under Art. 42A.2.1° and the express recognition of children's rights under Art. 42A.1 can ground orders that previously relied on the more indirect parental failure route under Art. 42.5
options.C: Art. 42A removes the courts' jurisdiction to make orders relating to State provision of services for children
options.D: Art. 42A limits State liability to only those cases where parents are completely absent

questions.explanation: FN v Minister for Education [1995] relied primarily on Art. 42.5 and the State's obligation to supply the place of parents when parents cannot meet a child's exceptional needs. Art. 42A provides a more direct constitutional footing: children have express constitutional rights under Art. 42A.1; their best interests are paramount under Art. 42A.2.1°; and the State has express obligations under Art. 42A.4. These provisions give children's advocates a stronger and more direct constitutional platform for arguing that the State must provide necessary services, moving beyond the indirect route of parental failure under Art. 42.5.

questions.question: The Child Care Act 1991 enables the HSE to take emergency or care order proceedings to protect children. Following Art. 42A, what is the constitutional standard a court must apply when determining whether to make a full care order under s.18 of the Act?

options.A: The court must find that the parents are criminally guilty of abuse before making a care order
options.B: The court must be satisfied that the child has been, is being, or is at risk of being abused, neglected or ill-treated, or that the child's health, development or welfare has been or is being avoidably impaired; the child's best interests (Art. 42A.2.1°) are the paramount consideration
options.C: The court must apply a beyond-reasonable-doubt standard of proof to parental fault
options.D: A care order can only be made if both parents have been given a criminal conviction for child abuse

questions.explanation: Under s.18 of the Child Care Act 1991, a court may make a care order where it is satisfied that the child has been abused, neglected or ill-treated, or that the child's health, development or welfare has been or is being avoidably impaired or neglected, or that the child's health, development or welfare is likely to be avoidably impaired or neglected if she remains in the care of her parents. Post-Art. 42A, the child's best interests are the paramount constitutional consideration in this assessment. The standard of proof is the civil standard (balance of probabilities), not the criminal standard.

questions.question: Does Article 42A create constitutional rights for children that are directly horizontally applicable against private individuals (e.g., employers, schools, parents)?

options.A: Yes — Art. 42A creates directly enforceable rights against all private parties
options.B: Art. 42A primarily imposes obligations on the State; horizontal application against private parties is indirect — through the State's obligation to legislate (Art. 42A.4), statutory provisions implementing Art. 42A (e.g., Children First Act 2015), and courts' interpretive obligation to apply constitutional values when resolving private law disputes
options.C: Art. 42A has no horizontal application; it is exclusively a state-directed provision
options.D: Art. 42A applies only in family law courts and has no effect in other jurisdictions

questions.explanation: Like most constitutional rights provisions, Art. 42A is primarily directed at the State and its organs. However, its effects permeate private law through: (i) the State's constitutional obligation to legislate to protect children's rights (Art. 42A.4); (ii) the duty on courts to interpret private law (including statutory employment and contract provisions) consistently with constitutional values; and (iii) the potential for constitutional tort claims where State entities or those exercising public functions breach children's constitutional rights. Direct horizontal application against purely private individuals generally requires the indirect routes of legislation and statutory interpretation.

questions.question: In ZH (Tanzania) v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2011] (UK Supreme Court), the UK court gave strong weight to children's best interests in immigration decisions. Does Irish law require similar consideration of children's interests in immigration and deportation decisions following Art. 42A?

options.A: Immigration decisions are entirely executive in nature and Art. 42A has no application to them
options.B: Yes — Art. 42A.2.1°'s paramount best interests requirement applies to court proceedings; for executive immigration decisions, Art. 42A.1's recognition of children's rights and Arts. 40.3, 41 (family life) inform the proportionality assessment, and the ECHR Act 2003 (Art. 8 ECHR) requires family life and children's interests to be taken into account
options.C: Art. 42A requires the deportation of all parents of Irish citizen children to be prohibited
options.D: Only deportations of Irish citizen children themselves engage Art. 42A; deportations of foreign national parents do not

questions.explanation: Art. 42A.2.1°'s paramountcy requirement applies directly in 'proceedings concerning the welfare of a child' — which includes judicial proceedings relating to immigration detention or challenge. For executive immigration decisions, Art. 42A.1's general obligation on the State to vindicate children's rights, combined with Art. 41 (right to family life), Art. 40.3.1° (personal rights), and the ECHR Act 2003 (Art. 8 ECHR right to family life) require that children's interests be meaningfully considered in deportation/immigration decisions. The Supreme Court (Oguekwe v Minister for Justice [2008]) held that citizen children's interests must be weighed in decisions to deport their parents.

questions.question: The Guardian ad Litem (GAL) system in child care proceedings provides a voice for the child. Is the appointment of a GAL constitutionally required in all child care proceedings following Art. 42A.2.2°?

options.A: Yes — Art. 42A.2.2° requires a GAL in every child care proceeding without exception
options.B: Art. 42A.2.2° requires that the child's views be ascertained and given appropriate weight having regard to age and maturity; the appointment of a GAL is one mechanism for this but not the only constitutionally required method — the statute (Child Care Act 1991 s.26) gives courts discretion to appoint a GAL where appropriate
options.C: GALs are only required in adoption proceedings, not child care proceedings
options.D: The GAL system is entirely statutory and has no constitutional dimension

questions.explanation: Art. 42A.2.2° requires that children's views be considered in welfare proceedings, but does not mandatorily require a GAL in every case. Section 26 of the Child Care Act 1991 gives the court a discretion to appoint a GAL where it is satisfied that it is necessary in the interests of the child and it is reasonably practicable to do so. The Guardian ad Litem (Amendment) Act 2024 provides for a more systematic approach but still involves judicial discretion. The constitutional obligation is to ensure the child's voice is heard in an age-and-maturity-appropriate way — the mechanism may vary.
